content:
Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization Shouyuan Hu 1, Jie Jiang 1, Junbo Wang , Jiahao Hu , Yalong Li , Pei Li *

, Liang Chen

School of Physical Science and Technology, Ningbo University, Ningbo, 315211, China

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:GO membraneSelf-healingWastewater purificationDye rejection

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: A B S T R A C T Cross-linking graphene oxide (GO) membranes enhances separation performance by meticulously regulating nanochannels and interactions between GO flakes. However, achieving self-healing for localized damage, such as mechanical scratches, still remains a crucial challenge in prolonging membrane lifespan and minimizing costs. Here, we have innovatively designed a reduced GO membrane functionalized with sodium alginate (SA@rGO), which demonstrates rapid and effective self-healing performance. For blade damages with a width of ~10 μm, rapid repair can be achieved within 10 min by the addition of a minute quantity of CaCl2 solution. Through various dye rejection experiments, it was observed that both the rejection rate and permeance of the damaged membrane were rapidly restored to their original levels. During the long-term separation experiments over 100 h, our membrane exhibited exceptional rejection stability, further underscoring its excellent healing capabilities. Element mapping via scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and synchrotron radiation-based Fourier-transform infrared (SR-FTIR) spectroscopy indicated that calcium ions interact with SA molecules to form a hydrogel network, effectively ’stitching’ the damaged areas together and enabling self-repair. Consequently, our selfhealing functionality significantly elevates the potential of GO membranes for nanofiltration applications.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: 1. Introduction Water scarcity is an unavoidable challenge due to the global popu­lation explosion, industrialization, and climate change [1–3], with wastewater generated by industrial processes further aggravating the scarcity. Among them, 17 %–20 % of the wastewater comes from the textile industry [4,5]. Typically, in the textile industry, more than 100, 000 types of dyes and over 70,000 tons of dyes should be produced per year, of which at least 5000 tons of dyes are finally released into our environment [6–8]. Many organic dyes are inherently toxic and resistant to natural degradation, posing severe environmental and health risks [9]. The discharge of textile wastewater has emerged as a pressing concern due to its substantial potential to contaminate water bodies, thereby jeopardizing ecological safety and endangering human life and health. Therefore, the development and implementation of an efficient and sustainable method for dye wastewater purification is crucial. Membrane separation technology has gradually become a key tech­nology in wastewater treatment due to its low cost, high separation ef­ficiency, and low energy consumption [10]. The membrane filtration process has contributed to more than 50 % of usage in water and wastewater treatment [11]. In recent years, the research and develop­ment of new nanofiltration membranes of various two-dimensional materials have yielded ultrahigh separation performance, particularly for graphene-based membranes [12,13].

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: ation membranes of various two-dimensional materials have yielded ultrahigh separation performance, particularly for graphene-based membranes [12,13]. The graphene-based mem­branes, characterized by their unique nanoporous channels, are widely used in ion separation, seawater desalination, wastewater treatment and other critical fields [14–16]. However, the swelling, delamination, and damage of GO membrane in an aqueous environment, due to the hydration of their oxygencontaining functional groups, can affect the separation efficiency and rejection rates [17]. To mitigate these issues, cross-linking GO mem­branes with ions, small molecules or polymers, as well as chemical reduction, can effectively regulate nanochannels and the interaction between GO flakes. This cross-linking strategy addresses swelling and delamination, enabling precise control of water channels for excellent separation performance [18–20]. In addition to achieving high separation performance of the GO membrane, another critical factor to consider is the mechanical and

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: Available online 8 April 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/bync/4.0/ ). structural stability of these membranes. Notably, throughout the water purification process, membrane damage can be caused by any vibra­tions, stretching, or scratching [21,22], For example, during the treat­ment of textile wastewater, nanofiltration membranes often face issues of damage and failure. Sources of damage include the particles that were not removed during pretreatment, the air or water hammers that generated during operation and maintenance, and the over-high trans­membrane pressure that resulted in the breakage of hollow fibers in membranes [21]. The lifespan of conventional commercial organic polymer membranes used in water treatment typically does not exceed 10 years, while in wastewater treatment, this period is typically reduced less than 5 years [23]. Approximately 840,000 discarded membranes are landfilled or incinerated each year [24], causing significant environ­mental impact. Additionally, the production cost of the original com­mercial modules is relatively high (ultrafiltration or nanofiltration membranes range from 400 to 800 euros per module) [23]. In conclu­sion, membrane damage and failure significantly reduce the separation efficiency and shorten the lifespan of nanofiltration membranes, thereby obviously increasing economic and environmental costs.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: reduce the separation efficiency and shorten the lifespan of nanofiltration membranes, thereby obviously increasing economic and environmental costs. One of the promising strategies to deal with membrane damage is to develop and improve the self-healing properties of the membrane. By applying self-healing membrane technology in textile wastewater treatment, it is possible to effectively reduce membrane damage and failure, lower maintenance costs, and minimize the environmental burden caused by membrane replacement and cleaning, further enhance the economic viability and sustainability of textile wastewater treat­ment. Actually, the self-healing concept and corresponding technology has been widely applied in biomedical and smart sensing industries [25–27]. Recently, it has emerged as a potential solution for membrane filtration in wastewater treatment. Kim et al. [21] developed a chitosan-based healing method to repair the damaged polymer mem­brane. Chang et al. [28] developed a GO composite membrane based on the dual modification effect of chitosan and metal-organic framework (MOF), which exhibits excellent self-healing properties. Yu et al. [29] developed a self-healing nanofiltration membrane based on the Schiff base reaction, and this chemical crosslinking significantly enhanced the structural stability of the membrane. These efforts provide a promising approach to prolonging the lifespan of membranes and enhancing their reliability in practical applications.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: embrane. These efforts provide a promising approach to prolonging the lifespan of membranes and enhancing their reliability in practical applications. However, self-healing nanofiltration membranes often require external force-assisted filtration during the repair process [28–30], which complicates the procedure, extends repair times (spanning several hours), and results in moderate healing effects. These factors limit their practical application in industrial production settings [31]. In light of these challenges, research aimed at enhancing repair efficiency, simplifying repair procedures, and reducing repair costs has become increasingly important. The exploration of self-healing technologies for membranes has become increasingly crucial, despite the persistence of significant challenges. In this paper, we prepared a self-healing GO membrane in which the GO flakes were reduced using a hydrothermal process with an ammonia solution and cross-linked with SA molecules, followed by fabrication into membranes using vacuum filtration. For blade damages with a width of approximately 10 μm, the repair process can be completed within 10 min by adding a small quantity of CaCl2 solution or by immersing the damaged area in CaCl2 solution. The SEM images demonstrate that the application of Ca2+ ions has results in the effective repair of the damaged areas, in comparison to the untreated regions.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: images demonstrate that the application of Ca2+ ions has results in the effective repair of the damaged areas, in comparison to the untreated regions. The results of SR-FTIR spectroscopy indicate that Ca2+ ions react with SA molecules to form a hydrogel network, effectively ’stitching’ the damaged areas together and thereby achieving self-healing. Through various dye rejection experiments, both the rejection rate and per­meance were rapidly restored, further demonstrating the excellent healing capabilities. In addition, the impact of the SA ratio, membrane thickness, and dye concentration on the separation performance was examined.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Materials Sodium alginate ((C6H7O6Na)n, AR), calcium chloride anhydrous (CaCl2, 99.99 %), congo red (C32H22N6Na2O6S, CR), acid red 66 (C22H14N4Na2O7S2, AR66), and alclan blue 8GX (C56H68Cl4CuN16S4, AB8GX) were procured from Aladdin company (Shanghai, China). Deionized (DI) water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ was used in all ex­periments. All reagents were used as received, without further purification. 2.2. Fabrication of rGO suspensions The rGO membrane from natural graphene powders was achieved through the utilization of two distinct methods: the modified Hummers’ method [32] and the amino-hydrothermal method [33–35]. The syn­thesis of GO was achieved by concentrating 3.0 g of graphite powder in a mixture containing 2.5 g K2S2O8, 2.5 g P2O5, and 12 mL H2SO4, which was then stirred until a homogeneous solution was obtained. Subse­quently, the mixture was subsequently heated in an 80 ◦C water bath for 4.5 h, after which it was washed with 500 mL of deionized (DI) water, filtered, and vacuum-dried to obtain pre-oxidized graphite. Next, 15.0 g of KMnO4 was added to the mixture of pre-oxidized graphite and 120 mL of H2SO4, which was heated in a water bath at 60 ◦C. Subsequently, 250 mL of DI water at a temperature of 80 ◦C was added for dilution pur­poses. The suspensions were then stirred for a further 2 h at room temperature prior to further dilution with 700 mL of DI water.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: ded for dilution pur­poses. The suspensions were then stirred for a further 2 h at room temperature prior to further dilution with 700 mL of DI water. 20 mL H2O2 was added to the suspensions, which were then stirred for 30 min. The suspensions were then left to stand overnight. The precipitate was subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm and subsequently washed with an HCl aqueous solution and DI water, thereby effectively removing impurity ions from the suspensions. The final product was a suspension of small-sized nanosheets of GO at a concentration of approximately 4 mg/mL, obtained by diluting the original mixture with 1 L of DI water. rGO suspensions were prepared using the amino-hydrothermal method. Specifically, we mixed 27 mL of the 4 mg/mL small-sized nanosheets of GO suspensions were mixed with 520 mL of DI water and 360 mL of 28 % NH4OH. The resulting mixture was stirred at 80 ◦C for 6 h followed by an additional hour at 90 ◦C. This process resulted resulting in the formation of rGO suspensions. 2.3. Preparation of SA@rGO membranes A solution of 15 mL of 170 mg/L rGO suspension was prepared by mixing with 2.55 mL of 1 mg/mL SA solution, resulting in a relative mass ratio between the two components of SA/rGO = 1. This solution was thendiluted to 100 mL with DI water. The solution was stirred using a magnetic stirrer at room temperature (25 ◦C) for 6 h.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: rGO = 1. This solution was thendiluted to 100 mL with DI water. The solution was stirred using a magnetic stirrer at room temperature (25 ◦C) for 6 h. The abundance of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on SA enabled uniform cross-linking on the rGO nanosheets through hydrogen bonding forces. SA@rGO mem­branes were prepared from cross-linked solution suspensions on mixed cellulose ester (MCE; 0.22 μm, JINTENG, Hangzhou, China) substrates using vacuum filtration under an applied pressure of 1 bar. The physical pictures of rGO membrane and SA@rGO membrane are shown in Fig. S1. Additionally, xSA@rGO membranes were prepared with varying amounts of added sodium alginate, where (x = 0.5, 1.5), where, x rep­resents the mass ratio of SA to rGO. For example, 0.5SA@rGO indicates a mass ratio of SA/rGO of 0.5. 2.4. Membrane filtration experiments The permeation experiment employed a dead-end filtration appa­ratus with an effective area of 11.95 cm2. All experiments were con­ducted at room temperature, with the experimental pressure maintained

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: S. Hu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 727 (2025) 124084 at 1 bar. The feed solution was 100 mL solution of 100 mg/L dye, and the filtrates were collected once the filtration process had reached a steady state. The permeance (Jw) was determined using the following equation (1): Jw =VΔT * A * P(1) where JW is the permeance (LMH bar-1), V is the volume of the filtrate (L), A is the effective membrane area (m2), Δt is the permeation time (h) and the P is the applied pressure (bar). The rejection rate (R) for the dyes were calculated from the con­centration of the feed and permeate solutions. The rejection rate was determined using the following equation (2): R =(1 -Cp Cf )* 100%(2) where Cp and Cf are the concentration of permeation and feed dye so­lution which were measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer (ALLSHENG, Nano 500). respectively. Three parallel permeation tests were per­formed to obtain the average values of water permeance and rejection rate. 2.5. Membrane repair experiments The SA@rGO composite membrane was subjected to physical dam­age through the utilization of a surgical blade. A standardized blade is used, and during the damage process, the blade is applied at a consistent angle and pressure to scratch the membrane, ensuring that the depth and shape of the damage remain as uniform as possible. The number of scratch lines is standardized to four, the length of the scratches is controlled at approximately 2.5 cm and the scratches are parallel without crossing.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: atch lines is standardized to four, the length of the scratches is controlled at approximately 2.5 cm and the scratches are parallel without crossing. In addition, the water permeability performance of these membranes was combined to assess whether the damage was consistent, and only the damaged membranes with an approximate permeability were retained. For the repair, add 2 mL of 1 mol L-1 CaCl2 solution in a dropwise manner onto the damaged membrane within the pressure-assist device. The solution should then be left to stand for approximately 10 min. Then, remove the surplus CaCl2 solution and rinse thoroughly with DI water in order to complete the repair process. 2.6. Characterizations The morphology of the membrane was analyzed using a SEM, revealing intricate details of its structure. Chemical compositions were rigorously examined through energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The morphology and thickness of the two-dimensional nanosheets were characterized by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). Interlayer spacings within the membranes were determined using X-ray diffraction (XRD). Chemical functional groups were analyzed using SR-FTIR spectra. The dye solution concen­trations of the feed and permeate were determined by micro spectro­photometer. The size distributions of nanosheets measured by nano particle size analyzer.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Characterization of self-healing SA@rGO membranes The SA@rGO membranes were prepared from a SA functionalized rGO suspension by vacuum filtration (details in the Materials and Methods). The obtained membrane was physically damaged using a surgical blade, and then 2 mL of CaCl2 solution was added for immersion for ~10 min. Subsequently, the membrane was removed and rinsed for repair tests (as shown in Fig. 1a). The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the SA-rGO membrane were characterized using SEM. The membrane surface exhibits a defect-free and wrinkled morphology and has a thickness of approximately 1 μm (Fig. 1b), which is typical for highly efficient separation GO membranes [36]. We also tested the surface and cross-sectional SEM images of GO, rGO, and SA@rGO films with the same GO and rGO loading mass, which also showed defect-free and wrinkled morphology (Fig.S2, Fig.S3). As shown in Fig. S4 and Fig. S5, the AFM image of GO nanosheets shows a thickness ~1 nm, while the thickness of rGO nanosheets after amine reduction is ~0.8 nm. After SA crosslinking, the thickness of SA@rGO nanosheets increases to ~1.1 nm, which is consistent with the literature reports [37]. The AFM and TEM images show that the width of GO nanosheets is distributed from 0.5 μm to 2 μm, whereas after amine hydrothermal reduction, the width of rGO and SA@rGO nanosheets is distributed from 0.2 μm to 1 μm.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: istributed from 0.5 μm to 2 μm, whereas after amine hydrothermal reduction, the width of rGO and SA@rGO nanosheets is distributed from 0.2 μm to 1 μm. By cross-linking with SA, rGO enhances the membrane’s hydro­philicity. The water contact angle of the rGO membrane is 47◦, while that of the SA@rGO membrane is 42.5◦(Fig. S6). While the water contact angle of the Ca2+-SA@rGO membrane exhibited an increase to 50◦, following Ca2+-induced self-healing. This phenomenon can be attributed to the reaction of Ca2+ with the hydrophilic carboxyl groups in SA, resulting in a decrease in hydrophilicity [38]. XRD analysis of different membranes reveals their interlayer spacing (Fig. S7): the dry GO membrane has an interlayer spacing of 7.5 Å. The interlayer spacing of the membrane was reduced following hydrothermal reduction with ammonia solution. After cross-linking with SA, the interlayer spacing of the SA@rGO membrane subsequently increases. The cross-sectional SEM image in Fig. 1c shows that physical damage caused by a surgical blade formed scratches on membrane and MCE substrate that are approximately 10 μm in width and depth. Interestingly, when the membrane was treated with a CaCl2 solution, the damage in SA@rGO membrane was repaired, and one can notice that the damaged MCE substrate still remains its scratched state. As shown in Fig. S8, the cross-sectional morphology of the undamaged area of the SA@rGO membrane after self-healing treatment was also examined by SEM.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: s shown in Fig. S8, the cross-sectional morphology of the undamaged area of the SA@rGO membrane after self-healing treatment was also examined by SEM. The undamaged area of the SA@rGO membrane maintained its two-dimensional lamellar structure after self-healing treatment, with no defects or delamination observed. To further explore the self-healing capability of the SA@rGO mem­brane, a damaged membrane was partially immersed vertically in a 0.75 M CaCl2 solution, with one half of the same scratch immersed while the other half remained unimmersed for ~2 min. Then the membrane was rinsed with deionized water and dried for SEM characterization. As shown in Fig. 1d, the dashed line indicates the boundary between the immersed and unimmersed regions, and the purple area indicates the region treated with CaCl2 solution. Elemental mapping of Ca and Cl (Fig. 1e) further confirms that Ca2+ and Cl-ions are uniformly distrib­uted in the immersed region, as indicated by the boundary. A compar­ison of immersed and unimmersed regions reveals effective self-healing of the damage in the SA@rGO membrane, rather than the accumulation and filling with calcium salts in immersing process, which is consistent with the results in Fig. 1c. We also tested the SEM images of two different SA@rGO membranes: one damaged by a blade and the other damaged by a blade and then repaired with a 2 mL CaCl2 solution for 10 min. As shown in Fig.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: nt SA@rGO membranes: one damaged by a blade and the other damaged by a blade and then repaired with a 2 mL CaCl2 solution for 10 min. As shown in Fig. S9, the blade caused scratches ~10 μm wide on the surface of the first membrane, resulting in structural damage. In contrast, the second membrane, after applying a small amount of CaCl2 solution and allowing it to repair for 10 min, showed the scratch of about 10 μm "stitched" together, successfully healing the membrane. These results provide clear evidence of the self-healing capability of the SA@rGO membrane after physical damage and subsequent treatment with CaCl2 solution. To illustrate the underlying physical mechanism taking place in SA@rGO membranes, the chemical functional groups were analyzed by SR-FTIR experiments. The brightness of synchrotron infrared radiation is two to three orders of magnitude brighter than the conventional IR source, this renders it more powerful to study heterogeneous samples noninvasively and with higher spatial resolution. As shown in Fig. 2b, the SR-FTIR spectra exhibit characteristic functional groups including hydroxyl –OH (3300 cm-1), carbonyl C––O (1725 cm-1), aromatic C––C (1620 cm-1, 1490 cm-1), imine C-N (1235 cm-1), and epoxy C–O–C (1070 cm-1) [34,39,40].

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: roxyl –OH (3300 cm-1), carbonyl C––O (1725 cm-1), aromatic C––C (1620 cm-1, 1490 cm-1), imine C-N (1235 cm-1), and epoxy C–O–C (1070 cm-1) [34,39,40]. After hydrothermal reduction of GO to rGO with ammonia solution, the peak intensities of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy groups noticeably decrease, while a new peak corresponding to C–N emerges, confirming successful reduction of GO [34,35]. After functionalizing SA to rGO, a new peak appears at 1405 cm-1, attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of -COOin SA [41], which was previously observed previously observed at 1601 cm-1 and 1405 cm-1. The addition of Ca2+ ions, the carboxylate peaks shift to higher wavenumbers, from 1405 cm-1 (1601 cm-1) to 1414 cm-1 (1616 cm-1) of -COOgroups.These shifts indicate coordination between SA@rGO and Ca2+ ions, resulting in increased stretching energy [42,43]. The Zeta potential of SA@rGO changed from -43.14 mV to -24.38 mV before and after the addition of Ca2+ ions, which also confirms that CaCl2 reacts with SA@rGO, as shown in Fig. S10. We also analyze x_SA@rGO membranes with varying amounts of added SA (Fig. S11), where (x = 0.5, 1, and 1.5). Similar coordination between SA and Ca ions was observed, resulting in increased stretching energy. Therefore, the SR-FTIR results indicate that Ca2+ ions react with SA molecules to form a hydrogel network, which effectively ’stitches’ the damaged areas together, achieving self-healing as illustrated in Fig. 2a.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: th SA molecules to form a hydrogel network, which effectively ’stitches’ the damaged areas together, achieving self-healing as illustrated in Fig. 2a. ’L’ and ’d’ in Fig. 2a denote the nanosheet size and interlayer spacing, respectively. The ’L’ measured using a nanoparticle size analyzer, is primarily centered around 500 nm (Fig. S12), while the ’d’, obtained through XRD, is determined to be 7–9 Å. SA is a polysaccharide that contains hydroxyl and carboxyl groups [44], crosslinks with rGO nanosheets via hydrogen bonds. When the SA@rGO membrane experiences mechanical damage, the fractured SA molecules exposed at the damaged site can effectively interact with added Ca2+ ions. This strong interaction leads to the aggregation and crosslinking of the SA molecules to form the SA chains or network [45], thereby effectively inducing the rearrangement of the nanosheets which are adhered to the SA molecules or chains, and then finally “stitching” the damaged areas together and enabling self-repair. This process restores the membrane’s performance, including its rejection rate and permeance, to near-original levels. 3.2. Dyes separation performance of the SA@rGO membranes To assess the self-healing capabilities of SA@rGO membranes during rejection operations, we performed dyes separation experiments (see Fig. S13 and Movie_1.mp4). Properties of the dyes used in this work as shown in Table S1.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: on operations, we performed dyes separation experiments (see Fig. S13 and Movie_1.mp4). Properties of the dyes used in this work as shown in Table S1. We measured the rejection rates and permeance for CR dye under three conditions: pristine, blade damages, and repair. As shown in Fig. 3a, the rejection rate decreased from an initial 98.9 % to 62.0 % due to the damage. However, introducing CaCl2 with a subse­quent 1 min–15 min restoration period, rejection rate gradually increased from 91.7 % to 94.8 %, 99.0 %, and 99.2 %, respectively. Taking a 10 min immersion as an example (Fig. 3b), during the rejection tests, the rejection rate recovered and remained consistently at 99 %. The corresponding permeance, which was 70 LMH bar-1 of damaged membrane, returned and stabilized permeance at 40 LMH bar-1. Con­trastingly, experiments using rGO membranes for rejection and damage under the same conditions did not restore rejection rates, with

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content:
S. Hu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 727 (2025) 124084 permeance remaining several times higher than that of the original intact membrane (Fig. S14). The dyes rejection experiments demon­strate a rapid and effective self-healing of SA@rGO membranes. We also tested the nanofiltration performance of the pristine SA@rGO mem­brane and the undamaged SA@rGO membrane with CaCl2 added

(Fig. S15). The rejection rate of the undamaged SA@rGO membrane with CaCl2 treatment remained over 99 %, similar to the pristine SA@rGO membrane, but the permeance was slightly lower. This decrease in permeance of the undamaged SA@rGO membrane with CaCl2 treatment can be attributed to the crosslinking reaction between

Fig. 2. Mechanism of self-healing in SA@rGO membranes. (a) The schematic of self-healing process in the SA@rGO membrane. (b) SR-FTIR spectra of the GO, rGO, SA@rGO, and Ca2+_SA@rGO membranes.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: Fig. 4. Three dyes separation performance of the SA@rGO membranes. (a) CR. (b) AB8GX. (c) AR66. S. Hu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 727 (2025) 124084 the Ca2+ ions and the SA molecules in the SA@rGO membrane, which results in a more compact rGO-SA network and affects the diffusion of water molecules [46]. In addition to CR, two other typical dyes (AB8GX, AR66) were selected to assess the repair and rejection capacities of the SA@rGO membrane, detailed in Fig. 4. The pristine SA@rGO membrane exhibited outstanding rejection rates and permeance for the three dyes. Following damage and repair, rejection rates and permeance were mostly restored to pristine levels (99.0 % and 37 LMH bar-1 for CR; 97.9 % and 29 LMH bar-1 for AR66, and 99.9 % and 38 LMH bar-1 for AB8GX). A slight decline in permeance could be attributed to concentration polarization during experimental procedures and increased mass transfer resistance [47,48]. We also investigated the effect of varying proportions of SA addition on the self-healing properties of SA@rGO membranes. Membranes were synthesized by crosslinking SA with rGO at SA/rGO ratios of 0.5, 1, and 1.5 by mass. The rejection rates for Congo red were assessed following membrane damage and subsequent repair. As shown in Fig. 5a, the rejection rate for the 0.5_SA@rGO membrane after repair was 93 %, while both the 1_SA@rGO and 1.5_SA@rGO membranes achieved rejection rates of over 99 %.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: ection rate for the 0.5_SA@rGO membrane after repair was 93 %, while both the 1_SA@rGO and 1.5_SA@rGO membranes achieved rejection rates of over 99 %. This difference may be due to the lower SA content in the 0.5_SA@rGO membrane, which could result in an incomplete repair of the damaged area during the reaction, leading to partial healing. In addition, the relationships between the gradients of SA contents or Ca2+ concentrations and the repair efficiency were also investigated. As shown in Fig. S16, when the SA content or Ca2+ con­centration was not enough, the repair efficiency increased with the rise in both of the SA content or Ca2+ concentration. The repair efficiency reached a saturated value of 99 % when the SA/rGO mass ratio was 1 and the Ca2+ concentration reached 0.75 mol L-1. The effect of membrane thickness on the self-healing performance was shown in Fig. 5b. The 500 nm thick membrane exhibited a postrepair rejection rate of 95 %, while the 1000 nm and 1500 nm thick SA@rGO membranes recovered to 99 % rejection rates. All membrane with tested thicknesses demonstrated excellent self-healing perfor­mance; however, the thinner membranes had an advantage in terms of permeance. For different dyes concentration from 50 to 300 ppm, as shown in Fig. 5c, the repaired SA@rGO membranes also demonstrated robust rejection performance. As dye concentrations increased, there was a slight decrease in rejection rates, due to concentration polarization [49].

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: robust rejection performance. As dye concentrations increased, there was a slight decrease in rejection rates, due to concentration polarization [49]. Long-term stability is paramount for membrane applications. As shown in Fig. 6, we performed an extensive evaluation of the SA@rGO membrane’s separation performance over a prolonged 100 h period following damage and subsequent repair. Employing a feed solution containing 100 ppm CR dye, we measured rejection rates every 12 h. The membrane consistently maintained a rejection rate above 95 % of the repaired SA@rGO membrane within the initial 60 h. A slight decline in rejection rate was observed after 60 h, which can be attributed to concentration polarization resulting from long-term operation in a dead- end filtration setup; however, the rejection still remained above 90 %. Correspondingly, permeance remained above 40 LMH bar-1 following repair. We also tested the long-term stability of the separation perfor­mance of the pristine SA@rGO membrane, As shown in Fig. S17, the rejection rate begins to significantly decrease to 95 % after 72 h. After 108 h, the rejection rate drops to below 90 %, while the permeance remains above 45 LMH bar-1. Here, the degree of decline in the rejec­tion rate of the self-healed SA@rGO membrane was significantly smaller than that of the pristine SA@rGO membrane.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: , the degree of decline in the rejec­tion rate of the self-healed SA@rGO membrane was significantly smaller than that of the pristine SA@rGO membrane. The potential reason for this may be that the crosslinking reaction between Ca2+ and SA mole­cules can effectively suppress membrane swelling, resulting in the improvement of the long-term stability of the self-healed SA@rGO membrane [46]. Compared to the literature reported (Table 1), our SA@rGO mem­brane has a significant advantage in rapid and effective self-healing, which can be achieved conveniently by adding a small amount of CaCl2 solution; it rapidly restores both the rejection rate and permeance. Current self-healing nanofiltration membranes based on Schiff base re­actions [50] and chitosan [28] often require external force-assisted filtration during the repair process, which leads to long repair times (several hours) and moderate healing effects that affect their practical production applications. Moreover, compared to other self-healing sys­tems, the Ca2+-induced self-healing SA@rGO membrane also enjoys more advantages. At first, both SA molecule and rGO nanosheet exhibit favorable biocompatibility [51,52]. The non-cytotoxic and environmental-friend SA molecule has already been confirmed safe for in vivo applications, particularly in drug delivery systems, wound dressings, and biomedical materials [53,54]. Additionally, the SA@rGO membrane exhibits strong scalability. It holds great potential for

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content:
Fig. 5. Dyes separation performance of the repaired SA@rGO membranes. (a) Effect of proportions of SA on the self-healing performance of SA@rGO mem­branes. (b) Effect of membrane thickness on the self-healing performance. (c) Effect of dyes concentration on rejecton performance of SA@rGO membranes.

Fig. 6. Long-term stability of the repaired SA@rGO membrane.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: S. Hu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 727 (2025) 124084 large-scale production while maintaining relatively low costs during the manufacturing process [55]. Finally, the repair process is simplified and efficient. This process does not require external stimuli such as high temperature or pressure, making it a sustainable and eco-conscious so­lution. The aforementioned advantages contribute to the practical applicability of the Ca2+-induced self-healing SA@rGO membrane, which has been shown to demonstrate effective self-healing, together with high permeance and dye rejection, marking it a substantial advancement in membrane technology. 4. Conclusion In summary, we have developed an alginate-functionalized rGO membrane that exhibits rapid and effective self-healing performance. By adding a small amount of CaCl2 solution within 10 min, the membrane can be rapidly repaired from blade damages approximately 10 μm wide. This self-healing process quickly restores both the rejection rate and permeance of the membrane, ensuring its effectiveness in typical dye separation applications. Long-term separation experiments over 100 h further confirm the stability of the membrane. SEM and SR-FTIR ana­lyses revealed that the excellent self-healing is attributed to the reaction of Ca2+ ions with SA molecules, forming a hydrogel network that effectively ’stitching’ the damaged areas together.

citation.title: Rapid and effective self-healing of graphene oxide membranes enabled by alginate functionalization

content: ling is attributed to the reaction of Ca2+ ions with SA molecules, forming a hydrogel network that effectively ’stitching’ the damaged areas together. The pursuit of self-healing materials has long been a cherished aspiration within the realm of materials science, especially in the context of separation membranes. Our rGO membrane functionalized with SA presents a significant advancement in this field. It not only boasts swift and efficient self-healing properties but also showcases practicality in its application, rendering it a versatile solution. The uti­lization of a simple CaCl2 solution to expedite the repair process within mere minutes offers a clear and effective strategy for addressing mem­brane damage and facilitating maintenance.
